participants.name: Sofia
participants.professional_persona: Sofia is a research lead at an AI startup incubated under the 100 Experiments programme, focusing on AI-driven predictive maintenance for logistics. She previously worked as a lead data scientist at a regional e-commerce company. She is an active participant in Tech x Singapore conferences and industry roundtables.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep knowledge of time-series forecasting, anomaly detection, and reinforcement learning for industrial IoT. Experienced in end-to-end product development from problem scoping to deployment.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to expand her startup's solution to serve the broader ASEAN logistics market. She aims to become an 'Industry Champion' that helps crowd in more researchers into sector-specific AI use cases.

participants.name: A (wjn7ha)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-wjn7hacp
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-wjn7hacp
participants.professional_persona: A founder of a small agritech firm that uses AI to help local farms monitor crop health and optimise water usage. He is driven by food security and sustainability concerns.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Hands-on IoT and computer vision integration for agriculture, plus business development experience with Enterprise Singapore’s SME Go Digital programme.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Plans to apply for co-funding under the 100 Experiments programme to develop a low-cost AI pest detection system for smallholder farms in Singapore and the region.

participants.name: A (cl5krv)
participants.professional_persona: A recent computer science graduate who transitioned into AI through the AI Apprenticeship Programme (AIAP). She currently works on a 100 Experiments project pairing her with public researchers to build a predictive maintenance solution for a logistics SME.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Strong in Python, data wrangling, and basic deep learning. Rapidly gaining hands-on experience in deploying real-world AI solutions for small enterprises.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to specialise in computer vision and eventually start her own AI consultancy serving local SMEs.

participants.name: A (5rvgrs)
participants.professional_persona: A mid-career engineer who switched from semiconductor manufacturing to AI after completing a Company-Led Training Programme under TeSA. He now designs recommendation engines for an e-commerce platform.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Proficient in reinforcement learning, recommendation systems, and cloud deployment. Brings strong systems-thinking from his hardware background.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Hopes to lead the AI practice of a fast-growing tech startup and mentor career-switchers entering the AI field.

participants.name: A (adluoc)
participants.professional_persona: A PhD graduate in AI ethics who now works as a responsible AI practitioner at a government-linked technology agency. She ensures that AI systems used in Smart Nation projects are fair, transparent, and accountable.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep knowledge of algorithmic fairness, explainable AI, and AI governance frameworks. Skilled in auditing models for bias and designing compliance checklists.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aspires to be a regional voice in responsible AI, contributing to international standards and advising on AI policy for public good.

initial_posts.narrative: As a research lead in an AI startup, I'm keenly aware of the resources available to drive our R&D. For 2023, with NAIS 2.0 just launched in December, I anticipate a strong initial allocation of funds. The policy states 'Over S$500 million committed' for AI R&D over 3-5 years, so an annual allocation of around S$130 million for 2023 seems a reasonable and robust start to kick off the strategy. The 'up to S$330,000 per project' co-funding for 100 Experiments is a critical and well-known figure that directly supports our work. Given the emphasis on 'investment in compute infrastructure' and ensuring 'reliable, localised access to high-performance compute', I expect a substantial pool of GPU hours to be made available, perhaps around 5 million hours for the year, which is essential for our experimentation. Finally, to achieve the ambitious goal of boosting the AI practitioner pool, a dedicated team of trainers and experts will be crucial; I'd estimate around 100 personnel to support the various training and mentorship programs across the ecosystem in 2023.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The deliberations focused on the essential inputs for NAIS 2.0's initial phase, with strong consensus on several key figures. Participants unanimously agreed that the maximum co-funding per 100E project would be S$330,000, 5 million GPU hours would be available for experimentation, and 100 trainers would be assigned to support the program, all directly referencing policy and program goals. Total funding for AI R&D in 2023 showed slight variation, ranging from S$120 million to S$130 million, with a central tendency at S$130 million, reflecting expectations for a robust initial investment to kickstart the strategy and build infrastructure. The discussions consistently emphasized the critical role of these resources in enabling AI development, ethical implementation, and expanding the AI practitioner pool.

initial_posts.narrative: As an agritech founder, I'm eager to engage with NAIS 2.0, especially the 100 Experiments program. For 2023, given the policy's late December launch, I anticipate the focus will be on establishing the program's presence and laying the groundwork for future activities. This means a few high-level outreach events to introduce the 100E program and call for problem statements. Actual matchmaking would be minimal, perhaps a single pilot event, as the process of collecting and vetting problem statements and identifying researchers takes time. Training workshops would likely be introductory, broad-based sessions to generate interest and provide foundational knowledge, rather than deep, project-specific training. Similarly, platform onboarding assistance would be limited to a small number of early adopters or pilot projects, as the main wave of projects would only begin in 2024.

The prior phase's commitment of 100 trainers and 5 million GPU hours indicates strong support, but these resources would primarily be deployed for actual project work and training in the subsequent year, with 2023 being a setup period.

My persona's interest in 100E means I'd be looking for clear initial guidance and access points, which would be provided through a few targeted outreach and introductory sessions to kickstart engagement.

The complex nature of pairing researchers with enterprises and providing technical onboarding suggests these activities would ramp up gradually, with only a few initial instances in the launch year for testing or early adopters.

initial_posts.narrative: As a responsible AI practitioner, I view the 'Activities' phase for 2023 as critical for laying a robust and ethical foundation for NAIS 2.0, despite the late December launch. The limited timeframe means activities will be strategic and foundational rather than extensive. For enterprise outreach, I anticipate around 4 targeted sessions. These would be high-level events aimed at introducing the 100E program and soliciting problem statements, with a strong emphasis on integrating responsible AI principles from the outset. This aligns with A (wjn7ha)'s estimate, as establishing program presence is key. Regarding researcher-enterprise matchmaking, the process of vetting problem statements and ensuring suitable, ethically sound pairings takes time. Therefore, I foresee only 1 pilot matchmaking event in 2023, primarily to test the process and identify initial high-impact projects. This also aligns with A (wjn7ha)'s assessment. For training workshops, given the policy's goal to boost the AI practitioner pool and the availability of 100 trainers, I estimate 6 introductory workshops. These would focus on foundational AI knowledge, data governance, and crucial responsible AI considerations, preparing participants for future engagement. Finally, platform onboarding assistance will be provided to a small cohort of early adopters or pilot projects. I estimate 12 such technical assistance sessions, ensuring that initial users are properly guided on the ethical and effective use of the AI experimentation platform. My estimates largely concur with A (wjn7ha)'s, reflecting a shared understanding of the initial, groundwork-focused nature of activities in the short 2023 window, with my persona adding an emphasis on responsible AI integration at each step.
